summary:
As of March 25, 2026, Canadian Government Bond yields are experiencing a sharp "bear flattening" trend, driven by a volatile mix of geopolitical shocks and a hawkish shift in central bank expectations. Over the last 30 days, the 2-year yield has surged nearly 40 basis points to 2.95%, while the 5-year yield has climbed to 3.14%, causing the spread to compress from 36 bps in early February to a tight 19 bps by March 23 (dipping as low as 13 bps on March 20). This movement follows the March 18 dual announcements from the Bank of Canada (BoC) and the U.S. Federal Reserve. While the BoC held its policy rate steady at 2.25% for the third consecutive meeting, the market was rattled by the outbreak of conflict in the Middle East, which has spiked global energy prices and threatened to derail Canada’s recent progress in taming inflation (1.8% in February). Simultaneously, the U.S. Fed’s updated "dot plot" signaled a "higher-for-longer" stance, with U.S. yields dragging Canadian rates higher despite our weaker domestic GDP growth (-0.6% in Q4 2025).

For our institution, this yield surge and curve flattening present immediate challenges to profitability and rate-setting strategy. The rapid rise in the 5-year yield (now trending above 3.10%) necessitates an upward adjustment to our 5-year fixed mortgage rates to maintain our risk-adjusted returns, even as the housing market remains fragile. On the funding side, the spike in the 2-year yield significantly increases the cost of our primary funding vehicles, such as GICs and term deposits. With the 2Y/5Y spread narrowing to its tightest level in months, our net interest margin (NIM) is under pressure; we are facing higher borrowing costs for short-term capital without a proportional increase in long-term lending yields. Furthermore, the strong correlation with the U.S. Treasury market means that as long as the Fed remains restrictive to combat persistent U.S. inflation (near 3%), Canadian yields will remain elevated regardless of our domestic softening in labor and manufacturing. We must prioritize liquidity management and defensive positioning as we navigate this period of cost-push inflation and trade-related uncertainty heading into the July CUSMA review.
